items.title: Dunkelweizen comes out like a stout
items.body:
Dunkelweizen has been my specialty and I've had some very good batches. My last 2 batches I saw no bubbles in the airlock ever, but fermentation did happen. I primed and bottled and it was beer but not a good dunkelweizen.

I don't like stout at all. I just considered those 2 batches to be batches that didn't come out the right taste. Then I'm thinking it tastes more like an nutty brown but darker and thicker. Then I realized it tastes like a stout. So I called my friend who likes stout and asked him how he liked it (without telling him it was supposed to be a dunkelweizen) and he said it was a good stout.

I brew in 2 stages, each is a bucket with an airtight lid with that hole for the airlock. So have it in one bucket for 2 weeks, then siphon to the 2nd bucket for 2 weeks, then siphon to the first bucket just to leave more sediment behind and prime, stir, and bottle straight from that bucket.

So what on earth is going on? Is my bucket lid no longer airtight and would that make my beer into a stout? (I'm not going to attempt any more dunkelweizen until I figure out what I'm doing wrong.)

@Rob 1) IDK 2) I do the one where you pour in malt syrup 3) IDK 4) All the hops went in when I boiled the wort 5) IDK 6) room temp.

items.title: What material is the shroud of keglands counter flow chiller made of?
items.body:
The specification only says "pliable polymer", but exactly what is it made of?

To be more specific I would like to know what temperature it can handle? Is it food grade? If it's food grade, to which temperature is it food grade? Ie can I run boiling hot water through the water path? And then use the water afterwards?

items.title: How do I fix my wine-making setup?
items.body:
This is my 2nd attempt at making wine. I watched a YouTube video and simply went ahead. Because of lockdowns and limited availability, I could not buy all the proper wine-making equipment.

Steps I took to create my white wine:

  1. Pressed 7 kg of green grapes and placed the juice and pulp into a large steel container.
  2. Added 1 kg of sugar because I did not have a hydrometer to measure the starting gravity.
  3. Covered the container loosely and stirred it once per day.
  4. After 8 days, I separated the juice from the solids and transferred it into several plastic bottles.

At this point I started reading more about home winemaking and discovered that many people use specialized equipment such as fermentation buckets, glass carboys, food-grade hoses, airlocks, siphons, and kegs. Some mead-making guides also recommend secondary fermenters, bottling wands, and auto-siphons.

My current setup consists of:

  • A 10 L steel container
  • Several plastic bottles
  • A kitchen funnel

I have now ordered:

  • A fermentation bucket with a lid
  • An airlock
  • A food-grade siphon hose
  • A glass carboy
  • Brewing yeast

My questions are:

  • Which pieces of equipment are considered essential for producing good wine or mead?
  • Is a fermentation bucket better than a carboy for primary fermentation?
  • When should I use a carboy instead of a bucket?
  • Are kegs useful for small batches, or are they mainly for large-scale production?
  • Do I need specialized hoses and siphons, or can I continue transferring the liquid manually?
  • If I transfer my wine into a carboy now, will it improve the final result?

I would appreciate advice on what equipment is truly necessary versus what is simply convenient. I would rather not spend money on hardware that won't make a significant difference to a 5–10-liter batch.

items.title: Calculating alcohol percentage after diluting with a sugar syrup
items.body:
I am making Limoncello, I have added 8 lemon zests to 1 liter of 96% alcohol and put everything in a big glass container.

After 1 month I started making the real liqueur by diluting the alcohol down with a sugar syrup. The sugar syrup consists of a dilution of 800 grams sugar in 1 liter of water.

I've put the big container on a scale, used the tare button to put it to zero. In all my silliness I've decided to use the scale/a weight factor in order to 'measure' my dilution - so I went on and added 1 kilogram of my sugar syrup.

I think that the next time it would be better to measure an amount of 1 liter of sugar syrup instead of 1 kilogram - but I am still thinking about this.

Now I am calculating like crazy, but I cannot really figure it out. The most simple and erroneous calculation would be to say:

1 L alcohol + 1 Liter/Kilogram sugar syrup = 2 Liter/Kilograms
1 L alcohol = 96%
2 L mixture = 96 / 2 = 48%

But I know this is wrong. I however cannot figure out how and why this is wrong. Diluting alcohol with water will work this way, I guess. But the added parts sugar to the water makes it hard. So the questions are:

  1. How much alcohol do I have in my mixture?
  2. How do I calculate alcohol percentages after diluting with a certain sugar syrup concentration?

Rather than just having an on-off switch I would like to have an off-slow-medium-high type of control or even a slide bar or dial like a dimmer switch for household lights. Has anyone ever done a modification like this? And if so can you share the details?

items.title: Brewing a NEIPA under pressure: magnet in dry hop bag is stuck to the tilt hydrometer. Should I open & remove?
items.body:
I'm brewing a NEIPA in a Kegland Fermzilla under 10psi of pressure, and I planned to have 2 dry hops without depressurizing or opening the fermentor. I have a tilt hydrometer in the fermentor and things have been going well so far - dropped from 1.06 to 1.026 in 4 days.

The first dry hops I prepared in a bag containing a magnet so that I can pull away the magnet from the outside and drop the bag into the beer. You can see this technique here: https://youtu.be/lvPBEwoTDPM?t=485

The problem is, I didn't weight the bag enough and when the bag dropped it pretty much floated on the top. Now the other thing that's floating on the top is my tilt hydrometer. Today I noticed that the hydrometer reading spiked back up to 1.048 and that the tilt is sitting alongside the hop back, so it's clearly stuck to the magnet. I'm worried the tilt ($160!) will get damaged by the magnet. It's still transmitting for now.

So the dilemma is... should I depressurize the fermentor, reach in, and separate the tilt from the hop bag?

This would give me peace of mind about the tilt and allow me to get a more accurate sense of when fermentation is done, but it would also risk oxidizing the beer... Given that fermentation is still active, maybe if I do it gently and quickly re-seal and re-pressurize it'll be ok?

items.title: What do you get if you distill mead?
items.body:
This is just out of curiosity...

I mean if you distill beer, you get whiskey... if you distill wine, you get cognac/brandy... if you distill fermented molasses, you get rum... if you distill fermented agave, you get tequila... if you distill apple-cider, you get calvados (if you happen to live in Calvados, France)... if you distill fermented horse-milk (kumiss), you'll probably regret it...

But what do you get if you distill (honey-) mead? Does it even have a specific name?

I'm obviously not thinking about distilling and filtering it into oblivion, at which point you'd end-up with (something like) vodka... I'm thinking about what you would get if you distilled it in a pot-still, wanting the base (here mead) to impart flavor to the finished spirit.

items.title: Distilled honey spirit needs an official name
items.body:
I am relatively new to the world of personal home brewing. I have always gravitated towards mead and my next venture ought to be distilling some.

I've read through lots of articles and threads on the topic and to no end have I found something agreed upon on the issue. Every other spirit has its own category based on its original ingredient, the process, or a slice of historical origin. Why on earth has distilled honey ferment not have a name of its own? Fermented honey is generally considered the most far back dated form of alcohol documented, yet the distillation of fermented honey is extremely uncommon for some reasons and there doesn't seem to be an officially recognized name. I really just wanted to give my vote and leave the conversation to others to explore.

If there were to ever be an adopted name for the spirit, I vote it be called Nectar. It has connotations to the source of the product as well as the mythos of fermented honey in Greek poetry "nectar of the gods". What a befitting name for the concoction in my opinion. I'd love to see what others feel about this name as well as the issue itself. Also, why aren't honey spirits really a common product at this point? I live in USA so maybe there are some in other regions that im not aware of.

items.title: Under fermentation
items.body: I recently tried fermenting pure apple juice. I didn't add sugar to reach my goal for ABV. I took enough juice out to use to make a starter, but my yeast was weak. It was close to expiration, so I added fermax and other boosters and rehydrated the yeast. After pitching this into the main must, I let it sit for 60 days to be sure all the sugar was used up. When I racked the cider, I checked the alcohol level and it read around 5% alcohol content. It has a bold, rich flavor because I used zero water, but it's weaker than I expected. Can I pitch fresh yeast to bring the alcohol content up to 10+%?

items.title: Reduce acidity of hard cider
items.body:
I've brewed some cider from cooking apple juice. The apples were unusually sweet for cookers so there was enough sugar and I added teabags to add tannin. This gave me cider at ~6.3% which tastes OK except it is very tart, like someone poured lemon juice into it.

I gather this may reduce slightly as the cider matures but I doubt it will enough.

What is commonly added to this type of drink to make it less acidic? Wine-makers tend to deal with acidity so are there tricks I can borrow from those guys? Maybe there are typical household ingredients even?

items.title: Ginger beer fermentation produces thick, white foam, becomes viscous
items.body:
I’m making a batch of ginger beer from a bug. 5l water, 400g white sugar. Within just over a day, the fermentation started foaming heavily. The foam has a consistency and taste of egg whites beaten for a merengue. The rest of the liquid is turning thick and viscous.

What’s happening?

items.title: Yeast raft or pellicle?
items.body:
Context

I picked up some mango "nectar" from Costco and decided to try making it into a gallon of mango wine. I pitched about half a packet of Lalvin 71B yeast, which the local store had recommended for this sort of thing.

I used a Camden tablet, waited over 24h, then hydrated & pitched the yeast. A day and a half later, and no fermentation. I tried racking it from my 5g bucket into a 3g carboy so I could see it and have less head room, and sprinkled more yeast on top. 12h later, still no reaction.

I hydrated the rest of the packet and poured it in. Another 12h, still no reaction.

I grabbed another packet of a different strain (Lalvin EC-1118), hydrated that and pitched it. Within 3h I could see something taking hold, but I also noticed these blobs forming.

Question

In the pictures below, are the surface blobs pellicles that I should be concerned about, or a normal part of the krausen? If it is, should I just try racking everything under them to another carboy for the remainder of the primary fermentation?

It's still very active (about 6 seconds between bubbles), so AFAIK normally pellicles would be unlikely, but with the opening, racking, and unclean air over it for a couple of days, might skew that.

Pictures

items.title: ABV question in 5 gallon melomel batch
items.body:
I started a melomel batch two weeks ago. Below are a list of batch details:

  • 5 gallon batch

  • frozen blueberries (roughly 4-5lbs per gallon)

  • 18.5 lbs of honey (raw, unpasteurized honey from my fathers farm)

  • 10 grams of Red Star Cote De Blanc yeast

  • pitched with 12.5 grams of Go-ferm

  • Staggered yeast nutrient addition (fermaid O)

    • 0 hours : 10.9 grams
    • 24 hours : 10.9 grams
    • 48 hours : 10.9 grams
    • 1/3 sugar break (roughly 96 hours) : 10.9 grams

  • OG: 1.155

Fermentation started really well. In about 16 hours it started burping out of the airlock. I "punched" the cap twice a day for the first week, and do it once every two days now. On a final note, I have a very sanitary procedure.

This is my 3rd mead, and my 2nd melomel. My question is regarding SG's and ABV. My OG started high at 1.155, and hit that 1/3rd sugar break rather quickly. I took a measure of the SG today and it was all the way down to 1.006! Meaning the %ABV is at 18.8! The Tolerance of Red Star Cote De Blanc is rated at 12-14%.

I took these measurements multiple times today to make sure I wasn't making a mistake. So my question really is: Is there something wrong with this batch that would allow it to go to such a low SG? Or is this just a happy little accident that will make this batch much higher %ABV than intended?

I know wild yeast can exist in raw unpasteurized honey so I am wondering if it is possible that is playing a factor.

items.title: The quest for the perfect small-scale kegging solution
items.body:
I'm new! :D

I've been looking for an answer for this conundrum for a long time. Maybe this is the place where all the pieces of the puzzle will come together!

On the surface, the issue is very simple: I'm looking for a reusable kegging solution for a small homebrew setup (5 gallon batches). The constraints: I don't have space for standard size kegs, CO2 tanks, or a separate fridge. All I have is half a shelf of the kitchen fridge.

So, in effect, I'm looking for a solution that involves 5-6 litre kegs (or smaller) that can be primed with sugar for carbonation and take a small CO2 capsule for pouring beer without losing pressure. This setup needs to either fit in a regular fridge or work with a separate device for cooling/dispensing (this is acceptable – I have some spare space on the counter).

There are loads of products that do this, so what is the problem?

It seems products generally fall into three categories.

The first category uses 5-litre metallic mini-kegs like these, which can be tapped with either a 'party star'-style tap like this and put in the fridge, or placed in a separate 'Krups-style' refrigerated system like described in this StackExchange question. The problem: feedback in homebrewing forums is very consistent that these mini-kegs can only be reused a couple of times before the lining starts to fail and a metallic taste is transferred to the beer. So every 23-litre batch would cost me about £15 in mini-kegs, which practically doubles the cost of a recipe.

The second category is very similar, but using high-end 5-litre kegs that can be reused indefinitely. The problem: each of these kegs plus tap system costs upward of £130 (here's an example from Dark Farm). So it'd cost me over £500 to buy 4 of these – the least I need to accommodate a 23-litre batch (and I'd still have to bottle a few). Also, the upright keg + assembly is very awkward for a regular fridge (that's from direct experience – I actually have one of the above).

Which brings us to the third category, which is PET bottles with 'Tap-a-draft'-style systems (this is mentioned in the same StackExchange question mentioned above). The problem: This appears to be very much a US thing – I can't find a reliable seller in the UK, and the few instances that I can find (e.g., here) use 8g CO2 cartdriges that don't seem to be readily available anywhere.

In a perfect world, good-quality kegs would exist in 5L size, making the first category the standout winner since I could get something like a Krups dispenser to keep my beer chilled and no longer have to fight for premium fridge space with my wife. But hey, we don't live in a perfect world, do we?

So, all the above considered... is there something very obvious that I'm missing? Has anyone tried anything different? Any words of wisdom would probably go on to make 2025 the best year of my life!

items.title: Using Refractometer to measure Carbonation Level
items.body:
I use a Refractometer when I brew mostly for the boil off phase of an all grain batch. This helps me make sure I get to the exact FG I want for the specific type of beer. It works well and I don’t have problems.

One day while drinking one of my Märzen beers I wondered what the gravity of my beer would be now. Even though I’d taken the FG before racking and knew it was on point. So I took a few drops adjusted for temperature and took a reading.

My FG for this beer was 1.013 - I racked to keg and then force carbonated. What was the reading of my beer now (?) 1.025

I was shocked. This must be a mistake I thought - I knew the bubbles would increase the buoyancy and so the gravity but that would have a bigger effect on a hydrometer surely?

I’ve subsequently brewed and tested all through the forced carbonation process and yes the gravity rose. I estimate that a carbonation level of 2.5 - 3 gives you a gravity reading of 1.025 - 1.035.

Has anyone else experienced this? It is an adequate measure of carbonation levels? Where could one find out more about the relationship between gravity and carbonation levels?

items.title: Creating a customizable high pressure extensions line for cga 580
items.body:
So I'm trying to set up a keg on nitro. The way my room is laid out, id like to have the n2 cylinder far away from the regulator and keg. One side of this setup requires cga580 male, the other side requires cga 580 female.

Because of the specifics of this setup I need the line to be a high pressure line that can connect directly to the cylinder.

So my goal is to essentially make an extension cable that I can place different cga 580 ends on. I've seen this referred to as a pigtail.

I can't figure out which parts will fit which other parts though. I bought this expensive cga580 male to male pigtail thinking I could remove one of the male heads but I can't remove the head.

Does anyone know if getting a stainless pigtail with 1/4 npt female ends would allow me to add adapters that are 1/4 npt to cga 580?

If so could someone point me towards the adapters that will allow this setup?

Or if not, does anyone know og anywhere that sells a cga 580 male to female pigtail?

Or any other ideas?

items.title: How to calculate alcohol without OG reading?
items.body:
I tested 'brewing' a cider for the first time a few weeks ago. Since this process was so extremely different from what I am used to with brewing beers I completely forgot to take an OG reading. To be honest the amount of alcohol in the brew usually doesn't matter but since the first question from people always is "How strong is it!?" I thought it might be nice to know.

This is a pear cider where the pears are 'centrifuged', liquid is then heated, cooled and then topped off with english cider yeast.

My first thought was to make a new smaller batch and test this, but don't have any more of the same types of pears (from own tree).

Is there any way to calculate the ABV without having taken the OG reading?

items.title: How to estimate alcohol content of a drink (without pre-fermentation data)?
items.body:
Is there any reasonable† way of estimating alcohol content, without knowledge of how the product was made?

Examples are a gift bottle of home-made wine, or a jug of fruit juice that has become fizzy.

† Non-reasonable would be such things as basing it on resulting drunkenness, or distilling it to extract the alcohol.

items.title: How to handle an contaminated beer brewed to use up old malt and post-contamination sanitization
items.body:
I have a beer that appears to have a pellicle contamination (my second unintentional contamination). The beer was a hoppy, high-alcohol ale brewed to use up some old malt. It is called "Hoppy Leftovers."

The sample I've tasted doesn't taste terrible, but a clear pellicle has formed on top of the beer. I have attached a picture.

My Plan: My current plan is to bottle the beer and see how it turns out.

Question 1: Bottling an Contaminated Beer

What are the potential risks of bottling this beer, specifically regarding bottle bombs or off-flavors developing over time?

Are there any specific precautions I should take during the bottling process?

Question 2: Fermenter Sanitation

After bottling this beer, should I perform any extra sanitation steps on the fermenter before putting it away?

Normally, I clean and rinse my fermenters after use, then sanitize again before the next batch. What is the recommended procedure for sanitizing equipment that has housed a confirmed contamination?

items.body: I really don't want to start a major battle over the use of Irish Moss in beer making, just wish to become more educated about the use of this product. I have reviewed the pro and con websites, that say, of course it's bad or it's safe. Is there any specific independent research or information online that isn't bias? I'm happy with my beer sans Irish Moss, so most will say I've made my choice already. Just looking for legitimate information to add to my knowledge base. Thanks for any insight.

items.title: Does anyone know of valved stoppers being available?
items.body:
Way back in the 70's and 80's I used to get my homebrew supplies from Boots the Chemist. I recall getting plastic (polythene?) wine corks/stoppers. They came in regular and "valved" versions. The valved version had a small (8mm?) "chimney" in the centre with a small hole in the side around which was a very small tight-fitting rubber band. Excess pressure in your (vertical) bottle could blow off via the chimney/rubber band without exploding your bottle.

I have had several beer bottles explode on me and would quite like to acquire some similar stoppers, but have been unable to find any. Not quite sure what to search for. Any ideas?

items.title: Should you mix the yeast into the cooled wort or let it float on top?
items.body:
I was told in Why is it OK to pour the wort into the fermentor as opposed to siphoning it WRT aerating? to mix and splash the cooled wort to aerate it when I start the fermentation. I poured the cooled wort into the 5gal bucket, then pitched the dry yeast in, then mixed and splashed the cooled wort for 5min. Now it's been 3-days and no activity at all, not one bubble. Was I supposed to mix and splash first then just drop the yeast on top? Or Did I do the right thing by adding the yeast first then mixing it all together vigorously. If I did it wrong what can I do now? Add more yeast?

Another tidbit is that I use "no rinse" sanitizer to sanitize my fermentation bucket, lid, airlock etc, but I always rinse it thoroughly anyway. This was the first time that I gave it just a quick rinse and there was a little foam on the brew from the sanitizer residue. Does the "no rinse" method slow down the yeast.

items.title: Do I need to decant my 1st stage yeast starter, prior to adding 2nd stage wort solution?

I’m making a lager.

Should I decant the spent wort from the first stage before i add the freshly boiled (and cooled) DME solution for the second stage?

Will not decanting result in off in final beer.

I will however, decant after the 2nd stage.

Thanks

items.title: Ginger bug does not produce enough carbonation
items.body:
I am using the following recipe to grow an active ginger bug:

2 cups bottled water 2 tbsp grated organic ginger 2 tbs refined sugar

Each day I add 2 tbsp ginger and 2 tbsp sugar to the mix. I wash all my utensils with bottled water before using. I use a plastic spoon for stirring. Temperature ranges between 22 C and 28 C.

After 24h the bug is showing signs of fizziness, but only small bubbles. This doesn’t change significantly after 48h. After 72h it goes flat and if I keep feeding it will eventually develop a slight film of mold on top.

If, however I start brewing the ale after 48h have passed (for syrup I use 2L water, 54g ginger, 273g sugar, 3 lemons, brought to room temp and mixed with 100g ginger bug liquid) it will not develop enough carbonation after 3rd day of bottling. It does have some fizziness but it’s not near to making a “pop” sound when the bottle is opened or have proper carbonation when poured in a glass. The liquid also seems sweet enough, so I would say it is probably not a case of all of it being eaten away by the bug in the ale.

I have tried numerous times and the outcome is the same: either flat bug by day 3 OR not enough carbonation if I use the bug on day 2 for making ale.

What am I doing wrong? Can anyone please help? I have read all sorts of advice and tried to mitigate sources of error having to do with ingredients, so I can’t tell what it is that I’m doing wrong, especially because everyone says this is the easiest recipe to get right :)

items.title: steeping grains for an all-grain brew
items.body:
I am doing an all-grain batch that requires a pound of 2-row brewers be steeped. I have two questions:

  1. how much water and how hot
  2. when is the steeped liquid added back into the process, at mash-in or before the boil?

I found the recipe on brewtoad. The original recipe called for the following: 1.5# 6-row 1.5# wheat malt extract (Changed to 2# wheat malt) 1.0# 2-row (steeped) 1.0# Carapils 1.0# corn flaked 1.0# Honey malt All the batches I have made were all ALL-Grain. None of this steeping bit. My first thought was to just toss it into the mash and move on. In doing some research on the steeping process it just entered confusion into the mix. As this should be a simple Honey

items.title: My apple juice is not fermenting
items.body: We have done two lots of apple pressing recently. One about 6 weeks ago and another 4 weeks ago. On each occasion we got 5 gallons of juice. So far it has not fermented at all. I used sodium metasulphite to rinse everything out and was fairly liberal with it, but not stupidly so. Otherwise we have added pectinase and yeast nutrient to both barrels. The SG has not budged in this time and the juice (on Sunday last) tasted of apple juice. I have used 6 packs of good champagne/cider yeast with one batch and four with the other (three different sorts of champagne/cider yeast) and I have stirred and agitated (I racked off into another barrel and back again to get as much O2 in as possible). The sulphite strips suggest there is about 10 mg/l remaining so, theoretically, this should not inhibit fermentation. The back kitchen is warm enough. The yeast has been sprinkled on the top several times and on some occasions has been encouraged to begin its fermentation by leaving it to stand in warm water with a bit of sugar added (I make bread, so understand yeast). I have given the yeast about a week to work on each occasion but to no avail. Anyone any other ideas as to what I can do to encourage 10 gallons of apple juice to ferment? We did not have this difficulty last year (our first year when we had a 'vintage' brew) so we are stumped.

items.title: Why are there raisins floating in my ginger beer?
items.body: When I buy homemade ginger beer (in South Africa), there are often small black raisins floating in it, about 1cm in diameter. Why did the makers decide to put them in?

items.title: What's the amount of psi a 5 gallon water bottle can hold?
items.body:
What's the amount of psi a 5 gallon water bottle can hold? My 5 gallon is filled with beer and a compressor is used to maintain a pressure. I do not want my bottle explodes!

I use a 5 gallon of water because I don't have a normal 5 gallon keg.

items.title: Black specs in foam
items.body:
I recently made a 10gal batch of ESB (which I have made many times). I took the following steps with this:

  1. I fermented it in a 14gal stainless steel chapman pot for 2 weeks
  2. Kegged it and tapped it to my kegerator for a week, but did not really drink much. It tasted okay (but not my best batch), not infected for sure though.
  3. Took it off the kegerator and left it in the pantry (needed to ferment another beer in the kegerator for 2 weeks).
  4. After 2 weeks of the beer sitting in the keg, I then put it back in the kegerator and tapped it.

The first couple pours I saw some black spots in the foam. I was able to pick them up with my finger and when I rubbed them with another finger they smeared black on my finger. I stopped seeing these black spots after about 3 glasses I poured out, but the beer seems to be tasting a little metallic to me now.

Anyone know what these spots may be or where I went wrong? I can't tell if they are from the keg (it was a used keg we bought), from the beer, or from the kegerator line (or tap?).

items.title: How to get Limoncello to look cloudy
items.body:
I have been making Limoncello for about 3 years. It tastes really good, but I can't get the cloudy appearance the Commercial product has. Looking at recipes for "Creme di Limoncello" (which uses milk and cream), I am thinking of substituting some no fat milk for water in the simple syrup.
Does anyone have an opinion about this, or tried it?

items.title: Is using brass ball valves safe?
items.body: I heard the other day that using brass in your brewery set-up is a bad idea?! Well I have brass ball valves from the hardware store on all my kettles. I know that typical brass has lead in it but is this type of brass the same? Is it dangerous? If so, how?

items.title: Can adding too much yeast to my sugar wash mess up fermentation?
items.body: I am starting my own vodka with a 4 gallon sugar wash. I added 10 lb of sugar and a whole pack of turbo yeast (for a 5.5 gallon wash). I read a little bit that adding too much might make the temperature rise and kill the remaining yeast. My wash has been sitting for a little over a day and has stayed steady at 90.5 degrees Fahrenheit. Will this mess up fermentation or affect the flavor of my vodka after distillation?

items.title: black lager and diacetyl rest
items.body: I am doing a black lager and was wanting to do a rest. I read the instructions wrong and placed it in the secondary. Is it too late to do the rest? Is the new added oxygen going to actually make it worse?

items.title: Rust protection for enameled sparge water heater
items.body:
I drilled a hole in the side of my enameled sparge water heater and installed a thermowell, but the rubber seal is on the outside and the exposed metal edges of the vessel are in water contact and rusts. This is clearly visible as orange rust deposits under the thermowell after standing with clean water for a day.

I suppose I need to seal the edges for the drilled hole with some food safe paint. But what to use?

Alternatively I could create a tight seal on the inside, where the nut is, but this requires sealing against the threads which is probably difficult to do effectively.

items.title: Does mild heating kill yeast, and how does it affect flavor in homebrewing?
items.body:
I’m new to homebrewing and wondering about the effects of heating my brew mildly after fermentation starts. Specifically:

  1. At what temperature would heat start to kill the yeast, and how hot is too hot for it to survive?
  2. If I heat it to pasteurize or stop fermentation (e.g., for a sweeter beer), what impact might that have on the flavor profile or other aspects like carbonation or stability?I’ve read that yeast dies around 120°F (49°C), but I’m unsure how gradual or instant that is, or if lower temps could still harm it. Any advice or experiences with this would be great—thanks!

The reason for this is that I want to mix my mead with other things later, but the yeasts reactivate and eat up that sugar.

items.title: Ginger Beer - How long can it be stored, can it be frozen and what do you do with the yeast?
items.body:
I have made my 3rd, 4th and 5th Ginger Beer brews today! The first one was a success, the second one I ruined it (used honey for fermenting and sweetening but didn't think it out properly) but now I have made a 3rd, 4th and 5th brew!

The problem is, I have made about 2.8 liters twice and one brew with 25 liters!

As you can see, that's about 31 Liters of ginger beer. I have heard they can stay in the fridge for 3-4 weeks if bottled correctly. Is there any way to extend that duration? Can it be frozen? And for the final question, can I remove the yeast in some way, before or after bottling?

To summarize:

  1. How long is the duration that I can store it, without it going bad?
  2. Can it be extended? Eventually by freezing?
  3. How do you deal with the yeast? Do you let it be in the bottle? Shake before drinking?

items.title: Using beer gas in a soda stream
items.body: There is documentation online on how to connect a soda stream to a large CO2 tank. I was wondering if this could be done using using tank with 'beer gas', co2/nitrogen, mix and would give the Guinness bubble effect to whatever you infused (coffee, water, beer,etc)?

items.title: What is the ginger bug, actually?
items.body:
Recipes for spontaneous (wild) ginger ale sometimes refer to the starter as the "ginger bug". What is the ginger bug? Is it just an ordinary lactobacillus; is it a type of yeast? Is it something that is all around us, or something that is found specifically on the ginger? What role does the ginger play: is it simply a flavouring agent, does it act as a preservative, or is it something special (for instance, a carrier of important microörganisms that aren't found anywhere else)?

(When I say "ginger ale", I mean a fermented soft-drink that uses a small amount of sugar as the fermentable base. I'm not talking about beer that is flavoured with ginger.)

items.title: Always bitter, never any hop aroma
items.body:
I love drinking hoppy pale ales and I've been trying to brew them at home. The results are always nice and drinkable, but not quite what I'm aiming for – no matter what I try the beer is always overly bitter and doesn't really have any hop aroma, or maybe only for the first pull on the keg. I don't get astringency, just bitterness. The color is sometimes a bit dull too. I suspected oxidation but I've done a lot to try and eliminate that risk with no improvement.

I live in a tropical metropolis where apartments are tiny (so no room for a fermentation chamber) and ambient temperature in my house is 30 degrees (86 American farenheits). I'll detail my process and a sample recipe below:

  • I use Singapore tap water, which tastes good and is low in all salts. I treat 30L with 1/2 campden tablet about an hour before I start; I usually add whatever salts and lactic acid brewfather tells me to to match the american IPA water profile
  • I use a grainfather g30 and latest fermzilla
  • Mash in at 66 degrees: 5kg pale 2-row, 300g dextrin, 300g amber, mash for 1 hour with recirculation
  • the pH paper I use always indicates low – around 4-5
  • I mash out at 76 and batch sparge
  • I boil for 1 hour, adding 10g Chinook at 60 mins and 10g Simcoe at 30 mins; I add a whirlfloc and about 10g yeast nutrient at 10 mins.
  • I sterilize the chiller and my whirlpool stirrer for the last 10 mins
  • I do a 20 minute hop stand at 90 degrees with 30g Simcoe and create a whirlpool with my stirrer and cordless drill
  • I cool using the grainfather counterflow chiller and transfer to the clean and sanitized fermentor in about 20 minutes. I can get the water to around 34 degrees.
  • I seal and shake the fermentor for 5 mins to oxygenate
  • I then use my c02 can to purge the headspace a bit and add a layer of c02
  • I pitch 1 pack of lutra kveik and leave for 2 days with the spunding valve open so no pressure; the tilt tells me I am finished fermentation within about 20 hours
  • I then pressurise the fermentor to 8psi
  • I sanitize the collection jar, add 100g cascade and 50g simco, then purge it with CO2
  • I then pressurize the collection jar to 12psi, turn the whole fermzilla upside down and open the valve to let the hop pellets all fall into the beer
  • I then turn back the right way up and leave for 5 days at 30 degrees with a spunding valve set to 10psi
  • I then put the fermzilla in the fridge and cold crash for 2 days
  • I clean the keg and fill it with sanitizer before pushing all the sanitizer out into another keg using CO2
  • I transfer into the CO2-filled keg using the gravity assisted closed loop transfer method, with beer flowing from the fermentor into the beer out line
  • I think put in the fridge and carb at 12 psi for 10 days at 3 degrees

What could be the cause of the bitterness and the lack of hop flavor despite that quantity of hops?

items.title: 1st Time Banana Flavor
items.body:
Wow...I just poured by first beer from a batch of Honey Cream Ale I brewed using BRY-97 West Coast Ale yeast. I kegged it a few days ago, and it is a pretty strong banana flavor. I tried 6 oz or so when I took the FG reading (which was perfect) and didn't notice any banana flavor. Cold crashed, added gelatin and kegged the next day.

What happened here?

I have brewed approx 32 batches so far, and no banana flavor in any of them.

items.title: First time wine maker
items.body: Good morning , I’m a first time wine maker and have a question about the wine I’m making , I made 4 gallons of scuppernong wine. I used 2 1/2 gallons of grapes, 2 gallons of water, 4 pounds of sugar and one pack of yeast. I let it set for two weeks then I removed the grapes and strained the wine, then added another 4 pounds of sugar and another pack of yeast. My question is, I’m into my 3rd week of fermentation. Can I still add potassium sorbate to my batch and should I add something else? I hope I didn’t mess up because I bought bottles, corks and labels for my wine. Appreciate any advice

items.title: How to sanitize aluminium?
items.body:
I use an aluminium counterflow wort chiller. To sanitize this I circulate the boiling wort through the chiller to clean and sanitize the inside

¿Do you recommend any acid or alkaline product for this? What can I do to make sure that is clean.

items.title: Lemon concentrate fermenting
items.body: I'm trying to ferment leman concentrate ph was at5.4 at the start using yeast nurthail and dady yeast gravity was at 1.1 it doesn't want to get started fermenting temp is 83 any help

items.title: IPA Homebrewing: Temperature around 15 °C = 59 F
items.body:
I'm doing a razorback IPA in my house, the house temperature is around 15°C=59F, the beer seem to be a little bit hotter but noting seems to be happening. I'm afraid about the fermentation process, so I have ordered an health belt, but it will arrive in the next days. I would like to know if the beer yeast would stop, with the heat belt it will restart again?

EDIT: this my first time making an IPA, I'm using "the beginner kit". I ordered the Razorback IPA package which includes: malt , specific Beer IPA Yeast, hop pellet IPA, Dextrose Brewing Sugar and Priming sugar.

Original gravity: 1050;

I pitched the yeast approximately 24 hours ago at a room temperature of around 15 degrees Celsius (59 degrees Fahrenheit) and the wort was not warm. "Nothing seems to be happening" means that the air locker wasn't boiling as usually do, never waited all this time see the Beer yeast activating. Anyway I placed a 5-liter jerrycan of hot water next to the fermenter. The yeast has started to work, but I'm eagerly awaiting the heat belt.

I would like also to ask, some tips on the hop pellet IPA, when should I put them in the fermenter?

items.title: Expired seamoss, need answers
items.body: Will expired seamoss harm you? Is it harmful to use?? The gel still looks like when i bought it, and shows no Signs of decay. But it’s about a week past the expiration date. Just wondering is it bad to use?

items.title: What is the best way to ferment a lager?
items.body:
I have been brewing for a long time, but we just made our first lager (a Maibock). It has been fermenting in a fridge for 1.5 weeks @50deg. I have seen many different opinions online about how long to ferment a lager. I have seen some people who will drop the temperature to just above freezing for a while. Some places have said to let it ferment for months.

This article was one that I was trying to follow, but I don't know what to take away from this. I don't really want to wait longer then 2 weeks on lagers, or I will just go back to ales all the time lol.

How should I ferment lagers?
